" CURRENT ATTAIRS.

The West British journalists, who are a tail of the
British Institute of Journalists, had their annual dis-
play of eating and orating last Saturday. Tony Traill,
the crusted old bigot-Provost of Queen Bess's Univer-
sity, was there, and utilized the occasion to boom
Trinity College. Tt will be remembered that Tony
imported & lot of English journalists over to his College
to deliver lectures as a sort of preface to a proposed
school of journalism. If Tony could establish such &
school he might hope to turn out & corps of West British
Jingo journalists.  As practical newspaper men we
looked upon Tony’s importation of seven English jour-
nalists as lecturers with amused contempt. .Tony gxus

“ not quite given up his designs on “ Irish ” journalists
- yet, and he expressed a hope that one Healy and a Mr.
Stodart, whoever he may be, would help him. He

wants a committee of ‘‘ leading journalists ** of Dublin,
Belfast, and Cork! *‘ Leading journalists *’ indeed:
thore’s a lot of leading in them! The West British
journalists of Ireland are a poor lob.

Certainly Irish journalism, or journalism in Ireland,
wants levelling up, but Tony Traill is not the man to do-
it. There is little or no career for journalists in Ireland,
for which fact they have themselves largely to blame.
As for journalism in general, is there not a lot of ability,
education, and character required to make up the
“ penny dirties *’ for instance, and these form a big
item in the much belauded English journalism? . The
Press has a great deal of influence, and in the main we
think it is a very bad influence. Ior the far greater
part journalism is only mechanism, and the dishing up
what will sell amongst a vicious and silly public. If
someone could succeed in giving all connected with the
Press of England—and of Ireland too—a good grounding
in the penny Catechism we could get on fairly well with-
out any special help from Universities. It is the
greatest calamity of this century that the position of
leading preachers and mediums for conveying informa-
tion to the world should be modern journalists. It is
““ tyranny '’ to be ‘‘ priest-ridden ’’: it is ‘‘ liberty ” to
take your philosophy of life from such gentry as, say, the
staff of the ‘‘ Irish Times '’ and those of the English
‘“ penny dirlies.”

We ‘don’t know how presumably fairly sensible
people can assist at functions like most public dinners.
The long streams of unmitigated nonsense and insin-
cerities that are drawled out and applauded surely are
enough to male an ordinarily honest man grieve. The
Protestant Dean of St. Patrick’s, in responding to some
toast or other, concluded ‘‘ by stating that, on behalf
of his brethren, he very heartily thanked the Press for
the benefit conferred on the Christian community.’”
And this when Treland, or at least clean Treland, is out
at war attempting to stem the evil and un-Christian in-
fluence of the ‘‘ Penny Dirties '’ and other jowrnalistic
sewage. Some people apparently would say anything
at a public dinner.

They have made a wise P.C.. of Thomas Lopdell
O’Shaughnessy, Recorder of Dublin. If he likes that
sort of honour it is, no doubt, just the sort of honour
that he would like. And having received an honour
one would think that it is up to him to entertain his
friends: but judging from a circular before us it is his
friends who are about to entertain him. In this partly
whimsical world men get superfluous dinners who are
not in need of them, and many who need dinners badly
have to go without. The circular before us is
printed on paper watermarked -‘‘ Bucksburn Ivory,’”
and woe presume that indicates a foreign malke: at least
we never heard of ‘‘ Bucksburn Ivory '’ in connection
with Irish-made paper. The circular commences with:
‘* In response to o generally expressed desire it has been
arranged, ete.”” We smile at that ‘‘ generally ex-
pressed desire.”” Tt is only one more pressing circular
for funds for a ' presentation and dinner’ to a too-
highly paid official. ~ The circular contains names of
several gentlemen who have already consented to act
on the committee for this utterly superfluous and un-
called for function. The first name, or rather title, for
the name is not given, is ‘‘ The Right Hon. the Attor-
ney-General, X.C.”" As we write we could not tell who

‘the Attorney-General is—such is fame. The second

name is that of Mr. P. J. Brady, M.P.. Now, we think
our friend’ Mr. Brady, who is a public man, ‘could be

562

- better employed than in organising an affair of this kind
for Mr. Lopcfell 0’Shaughnessy. Is Mr. 0’S. a Home
Ruler? Then there is Mr. J. S. McArdle, F.R.C.S.1.;
“a Gerald Byrne, Rev. J. Russell, C.C.; Mr. C. P.
O’Neill, J.P.; P. J. O'Neill, J.P.; William Mills
Forsyth, and Laurence Wickham, J.P. This Mr.
Wickham seems to be everywhere. It is a wonder
people have not better sense.

We are hearing a great deal now about our ‘‘ finan-
cial relations.’”” As between Ireland and Great Britain
the incidence of taxation is all in & muddle. There is
a lack of exact information, and the guesses of the
Treasury officials are, of course, taken with more than
suspicion by Ireland. What is called Irish expenditure
and what is Bnglish expenditure are questions decided
by no decided nor consistent rules. ~ The figures are
inaccurate, and the plans of deciding what is Irish and
what is not are unconvincing.

England has forced great extravagance upon Ireland,
and she has made a complete mess of the job. During
these many past decades she forced over-taxation on
Ireland, but owing to the mess that she made in the
impossible task of attempting to govern this country
one of the outstanding results was that this overtaxed
country was sending out hundreds of thousands of
Irishmen that she—the prospective emigrants werce
overtaxed during their rearing here—had the cost of
rearing to the United States. England was ‘‘ suppres-
sing *’ this country, England was compelling her to pay
overtaxation, and one resultant was that the’ United
States were getting hundreds of thousands of fully-
reared emigrants free, gratis, and for nothing. One
million Irish emigrants, full grown, were equal to a free
gift from Ireland to the United States of no less a sum
than £200,000,000. Between America’s suction and
England’s extortion it is a tribute to the grit of the
race that there is any Irish question to-day.

The salary rate of higher officials in Ireland is too
high, according to Professor Oldham. The professor—
and as he is a salary receiver he ought to know—
remarked :— .

If every Irish official salary above £300 a year had been
fixed at one-half its present figure, the very
now fill the posts would have been glad to accept them. The
money paid to the Irish Constabulary men is about double
what the same men could have earned as ordinary citizens in
Ireland. The officials are everywhere in Ireland by their in-
come, their social prestige, and their irresponsible authority,
the masters, not the servants, of the Irish people.

- Well, in view of this we may take it that Professor
Oldham considers that he is paid twice too much; we
thoroughly agree with him. And we are sure that £250
would be more than ample for our toy Chesterton, Mr.
Kettle. These, and other men, are extravagantly paid,
and add much to the difficulties of the settlement of the
Irish question.

The question of a Tariff need not hinder the way of
the control of the Bxcise and Customs.  I'or our parl
we would be quite agreeable to a provision that the
Irish Parliament, so long as Tingland remained I'ree
Trade, should waive the right to pub up tariffs not only
against England, but against any other country. Not
having power to put, up tariffs few Irishmen have ever
thought out this Free Trade versus Protection problem
thoroughly, and the legend has grown up that Treland

- 18 naturally for ‘‘ protection.’”” We deny that. We have

considered the matter, and if Treland had'the power to
pub up legislative taviffs to-morrow we would probably
throw ourselves into the fight against them with as
much vigour as we ever put into any other fight.. And
we believe we would convince Ireland too. We were
speaking recently to a returned Australian—one of those
mon to whom you cannot talk upon any subject under
the sun for five minutes but the inevitable *“ Well, in
Australia,”’ ete., etc., comes out. He was rather full
of ““ protection ” for new countries. We did not argue
the question with him academically, but we put this to
him and impressed him—Australia is no parallel for
Treland in this matter. Australia is a new country that
wants a population, and does not care \‘{hgrc ib comes
from so long as it is a white population: if; is touting in
England and Treland for a population. On the other
hand, Ireland has a population, and she wants to
increase it, not by importing from all points of the com-
pass, but to increase naturally from the parent race.
That must be a gradual process, and any undue hasten-
ing of Trish industries would defeat her ends. For in-
stance, we import nearly £2,000,000 of boots and shof_:s
annually: if we wished to force that industry by a big
tariff we could only do it by shutting out or retarding

. imports of boots and shoes, and inducing whiole colonies

of English and other boot and shoe workmen to come
over and settle in this country. . For our part if Ireland
is not, in reason, to be Ireland for the Irish, if the con-
tinuity of the Irish race is not to be assured here, the
fight for Home Rule would not attract us, and the cry of
nationality would sound fraudulent in our ears. With-
out any artificial protection we have, with Home Rule
and a fair field, as much industrial ground to.cultivate
as we will be equal to. Forcing industry will only force
alien immigration: Australia and the U.S.A. may want
foreign immigrants, but we do not. We want an Irish
nation, with emphasis on the Irish.

We would not only be willing, but we would be glad,
to contract in the Home Rule Bill that so long as Eng-
land remained I'ree Trade Ireland should not put up a
legislative tariff against her or against any other coun-
try. If we had the power to put up tariffs we do not
believe it would be used, but no doubt an agitation for
*‘ Protection ’’ would be raised by a few people. That
is the reason we would be glad to contract out of ‘ pro-
tection "’ in the Home Rule Bill, for we would thus be
saved from a futile agitation.

It is easy enough for scaremongers to cry out about
the financial position under Home Rule: we don’t hear
the Unionists talking about the financial prospects
under the Union. The outlook for England is one of
ever-increasing taxation to meet English needs and
social legislation, and the outlook for Ireland under the
Union is to have laws framed, wisely or unwisely, for
[nglish conditions and involving huge extra expendi-
ture, forced upon her. And, of course, with the extra
aws the consequent extra taxation would be placed
upon us also. Indeed, the financial outlook for Ireland
under the continuance of the Union is as black as ib
could well be, and the possible, though not, probable,
establishment of a system of * Protection’’ by Britain
or British industries, into which system Treland would
be dragged without any consideration for her interests,
deepens the blackness of the outlook before Ireland if
the Union continues.

avourites who have battened on the Union—are playing

A GARRISON DRILL PANTOMIME.

Scene—A backyard in Westmoreland Street. (Enter
Alf Fox, Campbell, Ardilaun, Brother Goulding and
others marching, with big sticks across their shoulders,
and followed by Captain Cooper, acting as drilt instruc-
tor.)

Coop. Mark time. Pick your feet up.  Left, left,
left, right, left, right. ~ Squad, halt.  Stand at
ease. Noble veterans and warriors of Ingland,
I'm afraid we must start again practising the goose
step. Your last movement was most unsoldierlike,
and not in-the least calculated to strike terror into
a Papist Home Ruler.  Alf Fox stepped off with
the wrong foot; Campbell knocked off Ardilaun’s
pewter shako in bringing his weapon down to the
charge, while Goulding gave his right hand man a
dig in the ribs with his stick when the command
was given to move to the right in fours.

Gould, Please, drill instructor, it was the other fellow
that hit me with the butt end R

Coop. Silence in the ranks. Now, warriors and
braves, I want you to set about this work of learn-
ing your drill with grim and deadly earnestness.
Faney in your hearts that you hear the voices of
the near future calling upon you to do deeds of
daring valour in the tented field, and lose no time
in trying to become veritable Germans in military
discipline. Ardilaun, look to your front. Steady
in the ranls there, Campbell. Fox, get your right
shoulder up. When you become sufficiently pro-
ficient in infantry drill T intend to bring in a few
donkeys here, and train you as cavalry, so that if
occasion calls you may be able to take a glorious
part in some Balaklaya charge against the Papists.
Think of that and——

Alf Fox (aside). Tremble. 7 :

Coop. Let your hearts swell with martial pride. Por-
haps some saved writer of the future may celebrate
one of your dashing deeds in immortal lines like—

achievements of Wild Carrion Crows have yet to be

of Fontenoy from England’s escutcheon of military

You have your wish, Jim Crow, he said; there are
your Home Rule foes.

Ned Carson almost smiles to see, so furiously he
goes.

How fierce the looks Alf Tox do wear, who used to
be so mild,

The {reasured wrongs of Romish sway has made his

Yes, think on such a grand possibility, and set your
hearts resolutely to learn your drill. Squad, atten-
tion. By the left, quick march. Left, right, left,
right. Get the step there, Fox. Move to the left
in fours. Tours, left. Now, then, Ardilaun, don’t
you know your right from your-left yet? Mark
time.  TLeft, left. Head steady, Campbell.
Goulding, look to your front. TForward.
(They march off amidst much staggering, and
stumbling, and treading on heels.)

A. M. W.

There is an institution in Belfast called ‘° The Rescue
and Maternity Home,”” Malone Road. This place,
tnrough its agents, invite Catholic girls about to give
birth to illegitimate children to enter ‘‘ the Home,"’
where they receive every comfort, etc. No Catholic
priest would be admitted to see them, whilst a still
worse condition is insisted on—viz., that they must give
up their infants to the Home, which means, of course,
that they will be reared up Protestants. Recently two
Catholic girls were rescued, with great difficulty, out of
this place and sent to the Union hospitals. Here their
Faith and that of their children will, at least, be safe
from the machinations of Proselytisers. Catholics,
both clergy and laity, ought to be on the alert and keep
a sharp eye on these ‘‘ Bird's Nests.”’

The * Irish World ”’ comments on the ‘‘ opportune
and highly amusing skit’’ in the LiADER concerning
“‘ Lady Gruggery.”” The notes in tne ‘‘ Irish World ”
on the matter wind up this way :—-

A series of pen pictures, mayhap playlets, upon this wan-
dering troupe of tinker-psychologists might be worth while,
in furnishing some amusement to us and, by way of reflec-
tion, imbuing them with a sense of the humour of the
situation.

‘‘ Pensioner Yeats amongst the Peasants '’ might be
made an interesting and entertaining study. But per-
haps these gentry are not worth much more nofice.

There is too much of the Dark Brother about Irish
and Catholic publishers. Rev. Dr. Coffey, in the course
of his recent lecture in Longford, said :—

What we lack most, I believe, is the art of advertising our
«Catholic literary wares, of pushing them publicly every-
where, of placing them more prominently before our people,
not onlv at our Church doors, but in our railway bookstalls,
and on the counters of our booksellers’ shops; thus bringi
them into direct competition with the objectionable litera-
ture we wish to have banished from amongst us.

How true' this is may be indicated by the fact that
during the year 1911 our advertising revenue from the
leading Irish Catholic publishers, Gill and Son, was 6s.,
and from Browne and Nolan nothing at all.  Could
Dark Brotherhood go further than that? Is it any
wonder that some of our publishing firms are national
calamities and general laughing stocks?

M.P., discussed clerks - in
Treland, however, is not the only
country that has too many clerks: the drift clerkwards
has some points of similavity with the drift from the
country to the tow We believe that the recruiting
for the clerk army is unduly heavy in Ireland, and it
is comparatively all the heavier as Ireland is only a
half developed country, and she badly needs industrial
workers and pioneers. There is one generazl and
natural explanation for the rush clerkwards—clerkdom
is usually a genteel job and it is under d oline. A
clerk is not a man who need have much initiative; he
obeys instructions; he is told to tot and he tots. For
this latter reason a in non-vigorous type gravitate
to clerkdom.  Simil as in a lower stratum of Society
youths join the soldiers, and become cogs in a machine.
The Intermediate examinationsand Anglicised education
naturally produce hordes wishing to be elerks. Intellect
and memory rather than character having been culti-
vated in the Intermediate schools, lads lacking charac-
ter naturally like a job where there will be alw
someone to show them or order them what to do. W
thirk in ell *“ civilized '’ society—that is, society where
the servant maids tend to wear hobble skirts and the
milk boys green socks—there is a tendency towards silly
¥y, and a symptom is an undue rush for clerk-
s of all kinds.

—that this is an agricultural country. TUnfortunately

We think that tilting at the schools and at our 8ys-
tems of education'is being a little overdone. Mr. Brady
had his fling at them in u reasonable sort of way, and

- generally we are in sympathy with him on the point.
But schools and systems of education are to a_great
extent symptoms of other forces, though. no doubt,
they also are to an extent forces in themsclves. A
godless people will inevitably shape for a godless educa-
tion. - As BEngland once had religious education we
may say generally that it was the people who broke
away gradually into godlessness, and that the schools
are tending to adjust themselves to the rather godless
people of that troubled and unhappy - country.

If our schools ‘were a national expression of the
nation’s dea of education things would be rather gloomy
for us, but the hopeful thing is that our schools have
been moulded by outside influences. We won't be
reformed and invigorated by our schools, but rather
as soon as we have power to look after our own national
business we will reform and invigorate our schools. It
wasn’t educational institutions in the United States
that made the States what they are: it was the States
made the educational institutions. And i we may say
5o we don’t think overmuch either of the Yankees or of
their schools. When we have responsibility and free-
dom we will do better.

We hear rumours of sectarian struggles for power
under the new Insurance Act. The ** Saved "’ are
never satisfied. It was not by accident that two out
of feur of the Commissioners are *“ Saved '*; we sup-
posed that was arranged under pressure: as a matter of
theory, if not of working fact, there are five Commis-
sioners, for the Registrar-General is an ex-officio Com-
missioner, and he is a non-Catholic ; in practice we don’t
suppese he will interfere, so we may take it that the
Compmissicners are half and half, though why they
should be half and half in this predominantly ** idola-
ter”’ country we cannot see. The impr 1 is that
this silly half and half arrangement materialises in a
half of strong Protestants and a half of tame Catholics.
In this case, however, though we have no doubt the
““Saved”” wirepullers secured two strong non-Catholics,
the two ‘‘ Idolaters,”” Messrs. Glynn and Maguire, are
not tames. They are gritty men, whom we feel confi-
dent will not allow themselves to be cajoled or bullied
by anti-Catholic pressure.

The Irish agricultural labourer is an important prob-
lem, and the importance of the problem tends to in-
crease. The Report just issued by the Department
contains a great deal of important information, and is
very interesting. It is pleasant to read that there is &
decrease in the number of migratory labourers, who are
accustomed to spend many months of the year working.
on farms in England and Scotland. They are attracted.
by comparatively good pay, and apart from that the
migration is established as a habit now. We would like:
to see this movement of labour stopped altogether, for
Treland wants the labour, and we hope it will shortly
pay her to keep it: besides we do not suppose this
roving over to England does the labourer any moral or
intellectual good.

In 1910 the number of migratory labourers is approxi-
mated at 18,500, of whom 500 to 600 were women, or
the number of women may be up to 1,000. Mayo
is the chief source of supply. Some . time
back we discussed some aspects of this agricultural
labour question, and asked how .was it. that strong
farmers in some parts of Ireland were not able to get -
this labour in view of all the crying out there is about
the scarcity of agricultural labour in the country. The
Department, we see, touched on this question foo. - Ir
the course of the report we read :—

It will be of interest in connection with this side of the
subject to refer here to the question of the employment in
Ireland of Irish migratory labourers. It is a striking fact
that, despite the difficulty which is found in many parts of
Ireland in obtaining efficient temporary labour during the
busy times of the agricultural year, there is no evidence of
any increase in the number of Irish migratory labourers who-
go for employment to farms in Ireland as they do to those:
in England and Scotland. A small number of Achill workers'
find employment in County Dublin, a number of Donegal
men go to help in other parts of Donegal and Ulster, and
similarly there is a certain amount of temporary migration
onch vear from Cavan to Dublin, from Wicklow into Carlow
and from Kerry into Cork and Limerick. But the amount
of such migration taken as a whole is small. It is there-
fore. well worth consideration why there is not more émploy-,
ment for this special class of western migratory labour' in
Ireland and whether Irish tillage farmers could not secure &
larger share of this labour which is found so valuable in

the best tillage districts of England and Scotland. Apart
from the question of wages, however, account will have to
be taken of the. fact of the many opportunities for lengthy
employment afforded in Eugland and ‘Scotland. Many of
the Connaught labourers who go regularly to England pass
on, as has been pointed out, from one district to another
to meet: the several.emergencies of; these districts as: they
arise, and so insure, judged. by the present standard of
agriculbural wages, large earnings. If in Ireland any suc-
cessful effort is to be made to secure the services of these
Connaught and Donegal workers thoy must bo assured of as
continuous or successive employment as they can obtain in
England and Scotland. Tt must be remembered that Eng-
_ land and Scotland, owing to the long established custom of
this' annual migration, are bettor known to thom than the
tillage districts of Ireland. And in such cases familiarity
is a considerable inducement,

The supply of agricultural labowr in Treland has been
diminishing, and not only diminishing in volume, but
to some extent anyway in efficiency. The efficient
labour has, in part, emigrated. It is not the dregs of
our population that' America gets: poor Ireland, with all
her other burdens, has to keep the dregs. It is well to
know that there has been a slight advance in wages of
agricultural labourers in Ireland recently. But the
future development of this labour problem is matter for
serious consideration. The Department Report re-
marks :— :

The position and the remuneration of the agricultural
labourer are in all countries matters deserving of the most
careful consideration. Farmers are face to face to-day with
competition which is very wide-reaching. It is not simply
agriculture which is competing with agriculture, but other
industries_which are competing with “agriculture.  Each
well-organised industry is trying to draw to itself the most
efficient labour, and the outlook is serious for such indus-
tries as are unable to attract to themselves and maintain a
skilled labour force. The town also is competing with the
country. It is a striking fact also that it is not simply in
the new countries that: there are complaints of a dearth of
labour. but also in the old countries, and from an opposite
cause. viz., that in the old countries the standard of wages
and the prospect of advancement are lower.

In Ireland the deficiency in the number of skilled and
trustworthy regular farm hands and the increasing cost and
difficulty of obtaining temporary agricultural labour present
serious problems for the farmer. As has been pointed out,
so much of the best regular and temporary labour is now
attracted to England and Scotland, where higher wages are
obtained, that the Irish farmer suffers. The remedy for this
state of affairs can only lie in one direction, namely, the
making of as good a career for the agricultural labourer in
TIreland as there is for him in England and Scotland. This
means. on the one hand, that as good work must be given

- by the Irish labourer as he gives in England and Scotland,

and. on the other hand, that as good wages must be given
by the Irish farmer as are given by the English and Scotch
farmers. The guestion is a difficult and personal one, which
only the Irish farmers and the Irish labourers: themselves
can solve. But in such cases the first step must be taken
by the farnier. who is in the stronger position and has the
larger stake. It is to his interest to have the most efficient
and bhest paid agricultural labour. The question of the
supply of labour is of fundamental importance in the pre-
sent situation of Irish agriculture. There are in Ireland
more than 165,000 holdings of over 30 acres in extent,
occupying three-fourths of the total area of the country,
and if the future of tillage on this very large proportion of

. the land of Ireland is to be safeguarded there must be an

improvement- in the work and wages of the labourer on this
Jand.

On December 18th Mr. Walsh wrote to the Registrar of
University College, Dublin. In the course of his letter
Mr. Walsh remarked that it ‘* is obvious that an at-
tempt is being made to penalise my client because she
deemed it her duty to report to Dr. Coffey the lady who
at present holds the Spanish and Ttalian Professorship
in your College.”” Mr. Walsh also remarked :—

T need not point out to you that neither the fact of my
client having made this report, which she has always heen
prepared to prove up to the hilt, if an opportunity were
given her, nor even the further fact that your President’s
attempt to pose as an authority on Modern Languages on
the strength of his five months” holiday in Spain, has been
received with such irreverent merriment (shared in,
I am afraid, by unkind College colleagues) in academic
circles in Dublin and elsewhere, is any justification for your
authorities to refuse to discharge their statutory duty of
providing lectures for those seeking admission to your

Miss Devine informs me that at the meeting of the
Academic Council before which she was summoned the very
stupid question was put to her: ‘What benefit did she
expect to get from the lectures of an incompetent pro-
fessor "? Of course, the answer to such a silly query is
obvious. In the first place, Miss Devine did not apply for the
lectures of an incompetent professor. She applied for lec-
tures in Spanish for the B.A. degree, and it was the obvious
duty of the College to provide a competent professor. But
even apart from this, lectures from Miss Degani herself
would be of great advantage to my client, inasmuch as she
must attend some lectures to be allowed to present herself
for the degrees as an intern student.

I have advised my client that, in the event of your per-
sisting in your refusal to provide her with lectures, she
should apply to the King’s Bench for a mandamus to compel
the College to carry out its statutory duty. This will give
her an opportunity of exposing the whole ugly business and
asserting her legal rights, and I have, therefore, to warn you
that, unless within ten days from the receipt hereof you
intimate to me that my client will be admitted to the lec-
tures which she seeks, legal proceedings will be instituted
against the College without further notice.

hard to see that it fell like a bombshell into University
College, Dublin. ~ On December 23rd the Registrar,
directed by the President, promptly replied as follows :—

Gu_vonnng; Body will meet on the 29th of December, and

Lovers of pianoforte music may expect a great treab

MAINLY ABOUT THE PRESS.

. Certainly the Press as an institution is being analysed
and scrutinised these days in Ireland—and it is about
time. The analysing and scrutinising will need to be
Irept up until an adequate inipression of the power of
the Press for good or evil, and particularly for evil, is
implanted in Irish public opinion. We say particu-
larly for evil, for though it may be hard for the Press
to effectively fight for the right, it is very oasy for the
Press to lead the people downwards.

Rev. Dr. Coffey, of Maynooth, delivered a lecture on
‘“ The Press—its influence for Good and for Tvil”’ a
few nights ago in Longford. His Lovdship the Most
Rev. Dr. Hoare presided, and he hit the situation off
neatly when he said:—

The estates of the realm used to be described in former
times as the King, Lords and Commons. Now I think the
estates might be described as the Press, and the King and
the Commons, because the Lords have not survived as tho
fittest, and I think that is the order of their power.

The following from his Lordship indicates how alive
he is to the significance of the Press as a force:—

There is no doubt that in Ireland in former times we
thoucht that God and Country were one, and there was no
need of our having anything like a distinctively Catholic
Press. and that when we had a National Press we had a
Catholic Press, because the two things went together. Is
that so now to-day? I am afraid not. I am afraid that
these organs even that took for their motto ‘ Cod and
Country.” ete., are disposed to leave out the God and Reli-
gion. That is very much to be regretted, and therefore I,
who distinctly disapprove of anything like a clerical Press,
am beginning to think it will be necessary for us in a very
short time, and my reason is: You have shut us out of every
place where we can form public opinion; you have shut us
priests out of the Councils, and out of the Boards of Guar-
dians, and out of the municipal Councils, and out of the
House of Commons, and the question is where do we help to
create public opinion? I am afraid there is no place where
we can create it in except to create di vely Catholic
papers. You may call it a Clerical Press if you will, but
something like that will be necessary.

=

Anglicisation and imitation have been, and are, ruin-
ing Treland. The weak Anglicised creatures in Ireland
have a craving to know what {the English are thinking.
Of course there are thinking Englishmen worth con-
sidering and worth reading; but, speaking generally
what does it matter to Ireland what England is think-
ing if you have a grip of the truth that in many vital
matters, in the matters that chiefly count, England is
off the track and is thinking into an inevitable morass?
It is for the Irish to think out their own conclusions
from their own fundamental ideas. We Irish people
have an outlook on life, on the meaning of life, and on
the end of life that is not the prevailing view of the
English people. If we were virile and self-reliant we
would think out our politics, sociology, literature, and
other things in consonance with our view of the great
fundamental facts—the aim and end of life. But our
view of life has become, shall we .say, sub-conscious in
consequence of our having .become so Anglicised and
so imitative. We are not virile enough to face life from
the standpoint-of our own convictions—we. are rather
too tame and imitative to really have convictions. And
so we are depending for our reading largely on what the
decadent English papers serve out.  As a concrete
instance. ~ What periodical literature will you find
strewn round in the waiting room of any medical doctor
in Dublin? Personally we could not answer that ques-
tion, as.we have very meagre experience of the waiting
rooms of doctors. - We only ask the question suspecting
strongly that each reader who has experience will have
to say that very little, if anything, Nationalist or
Catholic is to be seen there.

Rev. Dr. Coffey takes a rather-despondent,view of
the Press in Ireland, and we do not wonder. He
remarked :—

It is hardly an exaggeration to assert that the newspaper
Press! in’ geheral is managed at the present. day as a com-

.%o the weaknesses and passions of human nature in the
mattor it prints and publishes. It is hardly an exaggera-
!non ‘o say that no newspaper can hope to live and thrive on
its own resources to-day, unless it publishes an abundance
-ofinews that is silly and inane, or morbidly sensational, or
immorally suggestive and sensual; of foul and filthy crimes,
suicides, - murders, adulteries, divorces; unless it furnishes
columns: of so-called sporting news to feed the passion of
gambling: unless it takes care to identify itself with some
powerful interests or party; unless it knows how to cover
up corruption and wink at abuses; unless, in a word—for it
comes to this practically—unless it is prepared to pitch
principle to the winds and shape its policy by considerations
of mere expediency.

As for some of the duties of the Catholic Press, Rev.
Dr. Coffey says:—

A Catholic Press ought to be courageous in exposing, and
fearless in attacking, public abuses of every sort: national,
social, political, municipal, administrative. It should
scourge without mercy all those corrupt and dishonest prac-
tices that poison the fountains of public life, and such prac-
tices are not entirely unknown even among Irish Catholics.
Thank God they are rare, but that is all the greater reason
why the Catholic Press should be fearless in exposing them,
and thus keep the public administration of our affairs
straight and clean, and above-all reproach of jobbery and
corruption. I know no paper that has done so much for us
in recent years in this direction as the Leaber. Its
early article on ‘“The Policy of Oblige’’; its articles on
¢ Catholicism and Progress in Ireland,” its exposure of
filthy plays in our theatres, of religious bigotry and intoler-
ance in commercial life; its consistent advocacy of Temper-
ance—are but a few of the excellent features that will be
fresh in the memory of its readers.

The following concerning the license and influence of
the Press is to the point:—

Anyhow this large licence is a fact, perhaps an inevitable
fact; certainly one, which we Catholics need to be alive to,
however much we regret its widespread actuality. Let us
think seriously, at all events, upon these few plain truths:
First, that the Press in the modern world can have an
enormous influence for good or for evil; second, that that
influence is being freely used, the whole world over, for
-evil ; yes, for evil, often deliberately, often, no doubt, uncon-
sciously, but everywhere for the dissemination of error, of
literature that is pernicious, morbid, unhealthy, demoraliz-
ing ; third. that the Press can be utilized as a pulpit, a plat-
form, a school, a university, for the propagation of truth, for
the promotion of good, for the mental and moral betterment
of society: fourth, that since the bad and dangerous section
of the world’s Press is being allowed, and will be allowed,
practically full and free scope for its mischievous work, so
far as the State is concerned, this evil can be met and fought
and counteracted only by the maintenance of a sound, up-
richt Christian Press, and its extension among the masses
of the people; fifth, and lastly, and as an obvious inference,
that it is a duty incumbent on every Catholic citizen to
support and promote the circulation of a clean, Catholic
Press in his own country by every means in his power.

There may be prominent people engaged in the war
for a clean Press who yet lack an insight into Anglicisa-
tion, and who do not see the necessity of the Irish lan-
guage revival. If there are, now is a very good time to
enlighten them. But certainly many of those pro-
minent in the fight have nothing to learn in the way of
Irish Ireland. Rev. Dr. Coffey, for instance,
remarked :—

The evil can be arrested only by the combination of many
influences: a thoroughly religious primary education; tho
stocking of our public libraries with only healthy and
attractive books; the zeal of the priest in watching the sort

THE AWAKENING OF NATIONALISTS IN
RATHMINES.

1 AM pleased to be able to inform readers of the

LEeaper that our Catholic Nationalists are showing
signs of awalkening to the neccessity of organising as
never before. Twelve months ago I felt very pessimis-
tic on the subject. A superhuman effort on our part
had produced the greatest victory of the last general
clection—that of Alderman Cotton, M.P.—a short time
before. I expected our party would make a determined
effort to fight the Municipal elections held triennially
in the ‘‘ Model Township.”’ I found only one gentle-
man in Harold’s Cross willing to stand, and he had
to withdraw for want of support. The result was that
until 1914 the Nationalists of Rathmines and Rathgar
have no representation on the local Council, no one to
check expenditure, or see that candidates of our belief
seeking posts under the Council get fair play. In a
former number of your valuable journal I showed the
extreme disproportion of jobs held by Protestants con-
sidering their proportion of the population. To these I
believe the positions of Librarian and Assistant
Librarian have to be added; two new appointments to
these posts being filled by a gentleman from Glasgow
and a lady from Kildare. I see no reason for attacking
the Rathmines Council over these appointments; the
fault lies -with our friends for not fighting to secure the
patronage in question. I see signs of hope, however.
A branch of the Gaelic League has been formed, and
meets at 87 Rathmines Road, where very commodious
premises have been secured. Such well-known Gaelic
Leaguers as Dr. Conn Murphy and Dr. O’Kelly, ete.,
are at the head of this branch, and that alone should
augur well for its future success.

Next, a branch of the A.O.H. (Board of Erin) has
passed its first year successfully, and bids well to be
the largest branch of that organisation in the suburbs.
Great need exists for such a branch. It provides a
number of Catholics willing to stand together in defence
of Catholic interests in this centre of intolerance. It
will afford amusement and create social intercourse
amongst Nationalists. Lastly, by providing lectures by
well-known speakers on matters of current interest
affecting our religion and country, it will do excellent
lprop’j\gxmda work for the cause we have so much at
heart.

Next, a Catholic Women’s Social Guild has been
formed. TFor a long time past there was a great neces-
sity in the Southern suburbs of Dublin for a place of
amusement and a suitable centre of social intercourse
for Catholic servant girls. These latter, coming from
'the country quite young, often had no friends in town.
They were placed in a difficulty as to how to spend their

_IRISH COLLEGES—RUGBY AND HURLING.

A S ““Gall” admits that Rugby has given him
shoulders' *“ which may not be too bad,”” and
that, in accord with his first argument, his lower limbs
must be well developed, I am pleased that we are meet-
ing through the medium of the LrADER rather than in a
R\}gby scrum. Still I am afraid ** Gall's ”’ conversion to
Irish games partakes of the nature of the enthusiasm of
most converts and he is a bit hard on his first love, and,
with all due respect, a little prejudiced. His asper-
Slons on the scrummagers also hit the referces, and I
should like to see him appear before a referees’ society.
He likewise is strong on the military, but I do not
think Rugby was ever strong in military circles in Cork.
When I undertook to answer *“ Gall » I did so simply
from the point of view of one who had played Rugby
and Hm'lm_g, as to the quality of the character incul-
cated by either game. My object was to controvert
the statement that Rugby had less moral effect than
hurling. That was my sole end in writing, and not to
debate the question from the puvely Irish point of
view. However, I may say at once that I should like
to see hurling adopted as ‘the chief game in every Irish
college, and my reason for so wishing is that Irish games
would assist in moulding and developing what little is
left of our real national spivit as distinguished from that
** patriotism ’ which: is alw eady to die in the last
ditch. So far I am a Protectionist.

My friend will forgive me if I say that I think he has
wandered from his first point, and in this I am strength-
ened by the more embracing title accorded his article
in last week’s issue.

I am handicapped in the fact that I live in England,
so cannot speak of the new spirit which imbues the
rising generation of school boys, but I can assure
““ Gall ™ that no one will be more pleased than I when
the day comes that the middle-class youths of Cork will
not be ashamed of being seen with hurleys in their
hands. Was any reason given by the Christian College
authorities for vetoing Irish games? I suppose it is
open to any school to impose its own rules as to study
and games, but surely public opinion can bring any
school authority, to recognise that there is an Irish way
of holding one’s head high just as much as there is the
way of snobbery. And, after all, who are the Christian
Brothers? Are they not sprung from the people?
How many of them were born in the purple that they
should fear the touch of a hurley would make plebians
of the high patrician youngsters who bask in the protec-
tion of St. Patrick’s Place? It is a sad story at this
time of day to think that a school with such possibilities
for the development of Irish Ireland should be not only
dormant, but even antagonistic. Perhaps the LEADER
can obtain from the Presentation Brothers their reasons
for encouraging Irish games and from the Christian
Brothers their reasons for the veto. Undoubtedly the
articles would be interesting.

January 20, 1912,

I think *“ Gall ”” touches the spot when he mentions
the burriers of detestable tradition inculcated by our
presont educational system. Cannob these barriers be
broken down? At least cannot they be penctrated here
and there? Surely the Gaelic League is strong enough
to have a voice in the educational question, and per-
haps a little ‘businesslike encouragement from the
@.A.A. for Irish games in Irish schools might do some-
thing to nelp on the good work. ‘At a boo](svtflll the
other day I picked up a copy of ** Handy Andy,” and a
sentence in the preface by Samuel Lover struck me
that it might well be applied to Irish games. Lover
had been aceused of giving flattering portraits of his
countrymen, and his reply in the preface mentioned
above was: ** That I love my country dearly I acknow-
ledge, and I am sure every Iinglishman will respect me
the more for loving mine, wnen he is, with justice,
proud of his.”’ : i

I trust ** Gall”’ will not despair in ploughing his
lonely furrow, and he will find that others will come ere

the sun goes down to harrow what he has ploughed.

THE OLD TALE.

O’er the hills the lover came
When the west was all aflame,
Whistling softly down the vale—
Dreaming of the old, old tale—
Of a colleen whose dear hand
Lay in his through fairyland;
She had said she’d be his bride
If he braved her father’s pride.

“ Poor my land and small my flock,
But I come of olden stock

Who for Ireland fought and died—
1 have naught but honest pride.””

““ Not for you my daughter’s hand;
Him she weds who has most land ;
Barns and byres for her I claim—
‘What care I for honour’s name!”’

O’er the hills the lover went,

Pale his face, his proud head bent;
Dark the west, the sun had gone,
-And he felt alone, alone.

Where she dwelt a light shone far,
It seemed like a far-off star.

Dim his eye gazed o’er the vale

As he sighed, ‘‘ The old, old tale.”

But the maiden dressed her hair
When a ““ match "’ made at the fair
Gave her barns, and byres, and land—
Now a sleeveen claims her hand.

Yet, her eyes will sometimes fill

TFor the lover o’er the hill,

And her cheek will sometimes pale
As she sighs, *‘ The old, old tale.”

The Clonmel Gas Managership.

DEar Sir,—As no doubt you are already aware, the
above mz\n.melh]np is now hanging fire, and from the
tone of the letters which have uhc.uly qppemed on the
matter, it seems anything but encouraging for Irish
candidates,

1t was stated by the Chairman of the Gas Committee
that the selection of the candidate would be left to the
Consulting Engineer, and I presume this gentleman
happens to be an Englishman.

I remember some time ago the same Gas Committee
advertised for an assistant gas manager, and appomted
an Inglish applicant. This "eutlcm'm has since left,
and gone home to his own countr}'.

Has the Clonmel Gas Committee any duty to per-
form as Irishmen when they adopt an English engineer
and an English assistant manager ?

I know of one gentleman managing and engineering
a Gas Company in the South of Ireland, and within a
stone-throw of Clonmel, whose advice has been sought
in the selection of gas managers for Trish Gas Works,
and those concerns have profited by the appointments
they have made.

No doubt the Clonmel Gas Committee would like to
he a manager with a ‘‘ swank and accent,” as these
are necessary qualifications for the position.

Surely it is nearly time for the Irish people to wake
up, and especially concerns which are controlled by
Trishmen, such as the Clonmel Gas Works, to have some
confidence in their own countrymen instead of appoint-
ing foreigners to such positions when there are plenty
of men fully qualified to be had at home.

It will be a standing memorial to the inconsistency
of the Clonmel Gas Committee should they adopt a
foreigner to the position, and it behoves the people of
Clonmel and its Cor poration to prevent another impor-
tation—a town which can boast of such advanced
Nationalists.—Yours truly, OBSERVER.
